title: Transition from Collective to Local Radial Motional Modes in a Tapered Paul Trap
authors: Manika Bhardwaj, Moritz Göb, Bogomila S. Nikolova, Bernd Bauerhenne, Peter A. Ivanov, Kilian Singer
abstract: With coupled detuned oscillators, either individual or collective oscillations are observable. The latter is used in quantum information processing in linear Paul traps. Here, we study the transition from collective radial modes at stronger axial confinements into individual radial oscillations at low axial confinements in a tapered Paul trap. The eigenmodes are experimentally studied in detail in the transition regime and compared with theoretical predictions. The features studied will enable investigation of modified heat transport phenomena and defect formation in trapped ions.
summary: This paper demonstrates how the geometry of a tapered Paul trap can be used to tune the coupling between ions, driving a transition from independent local oscillations to collective modes. By varying axial confinement, the researchers mapped the transformation of radial eigenmodes. This provides a platform for studying controlled quantum transport and defect formation in ion chains.
main_problem: Investigating the transition from collective radial motional modes to individual (local) radial oscillations in a tapered Paul trap by varying axial confinement.
main_result: The researchers experimentally observed a complete transformation of radial eigenfrequencies and eigenvectors, demonstrating that increasing axial confinement reduces frequency gaps and enables the transition from localized to collective phonon behavior.
method: The team used sinusoidal modulation of laser intensity to drive oscillations and an iCCD camera for detection, comparing experimental radial eigenfrequencies and eigenvectors with a theoretical model based on a Taylor approximation of the trap potential.
model_or_system: A tapered Paul trap containing three 40Ca+ ions, where the trap geometry creates an axial position-dependent radial confinement, allowing for a transition between decoupled and coupled regimes.
key_observables: Radial eigenfrequencies, eigenmode eigenvectors (ion participation), and oscillation amplitudes.
important_parameters: Axial trapping frequency (47 kHz to 205 kHz), radial trapping frequency (~1 MHz), and the funnel length (1.81 mm).
main_assumptions: The theoretical model uses a Taylor approximation of the potential around equilibrium positions and assumes a 1D chain of ions.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the trap schematic and examples of local excitations; Fig 2 shows the progression of oscillations from local to collective; Fig 3 plots radial eigenfrequencies vs. axial confinement; Fig 4 shows eigenvector components (ion participation) across the transition.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the tapered trap geometry, describes the experimental setup and measurement protocol, presents the results of the mode transition and frequency divergence, compares findings to theoretical predictions, and discusses implications for quantum transport and nonlinear dynamics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for studying phonon localization, modified heat transport, and the development of nonlinear radial modes for continuous-variable quantum computing in ion-based systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic depiction of the experiment. The ta- pered ion trap consists of two pairs of rf electrodes, here shown in red and blue, supplied with an rf voltage with a relative phase of π. Axially, the ions are confined by dc voltages to the golden endcap electrodes. The spatially broad beam de- picted in light blue is used for imaging and excitation of the motion of all three calcium ions. The additional magenta narrow beam depicts the focused beam used to excite the middle ion. Both lasers are close to the Doppler transition of 40Ca+ at 397 nm. Additionally a repumper at 866 nm is aligned through the hollow center of the endcap electrodes, here depicted in light red. The inset...

FIG. 2. Illustration of radial eigenmode oscillations of a ta- pered trap at axial confinements of 40 kHz (a), 125 kHz (b), and 200 kHz (c), compared to the radial eigenmode oscilla- tions of a linear trap (d). (e) Plot of the sum of the oscilla- tion amplitudes of the three ions at each ωd over the measured range of ωz, while the middle ion is driven with the intensity- modulated focused beam. The outer ions couple to the middle ion with increasing ωz, exciting oscillations at different eigen- frequencies.

FIG. 3. Radial eigenmode frequencies Ωρ of the three ions at different axial confinements ωz. The squares and circles repre- sent the broad and focused beam measurements respectively, with the error bars denoting the standard deviation. The upper blue (middle green, lower red) solid lines depict the theoretical predictions of the highest (middle, lowest) eigen- frequency of the three ion crystal. At low axial confinements, the colors correspond to the local oscillations of each ion as depicted in Fig. 1. The gray dashed lines illustrate the eigen- modes for a linear Paul trap.

FIG. 4. The eigenvector components of the three ions at dif- ferent axial confinements ωz. Blue stars, green dots and red squares are the experimental values of the eigenvector compo- nents of the ion on the apex of the taper, middle ion and the ion on the open side of the taper, respectively. The blue-solid, green-dashed and red-dotted lines are the theoretical values of the eigenvector components, corresponding to the respective ions (Fig. 1). At higher axial confinements the zigzag (a), the rocking (b) and the COM radial modes (c) are observed.
